title: Literature Review
text:
A major change in perspective is taking place in the field of credit risk management, by adding a dynamic Machine Learning (ML) frameworks that prioritize regulatory interpretability and prediction accuracy replacing static statistical scoring system.
In the past, logistic modeling and other basic linear models were used for evaluating risk from credit score or assessment . However, Bussmann et al.┬а(2020)[1] suggested that modern artificial intelligence models greatly improved the ability to recognize complicated, dynamic trends in financial organizations. Bussmann et al.┬а(2020)[1] highlighted that these models need to be explained so as to be actually used in handling risks. They demonstrate how model outcomes can be split down into specific feature components by using statistical techniques like SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations), enabling management to validate the theories and at the same time derive the choices regarding credit. Shreya and Pathak (2025)[2] support this by emphasizing that XAI is the main link between the efficiency of ML and the integrity standards of modern banking institutions.
The use of XAI is a solution to worldwide regulations rather than this current technological solution. In their research on the use of XAI in banks, De Lange et al.┬а(2022)[3] pointed out that it is technically challenging to utilize transparent modelling for credit rejections due to the тАЬobligation to explanationтАЭ required by standards like the General Data Protection Regulation as per the the laws. According to their study, while collaborative methods like Gradient Boosting deliver high AUC ratings, they need specific explanatory tools (like Waterfall , Agile or Impact charts) trying to provide consumers and the banking industry the fine-grained insights they need for credit score processing
A diverse approach in the modern world that integrates quantitative data science with business goals and strategy is increasingly being used to analyze current consumerтАЩs evaluations of credit. Oyeyemi et al.┬а(2025)[4] support a comprehensive approach in which XAI is a fundamental part of organizational analytics instead of only a post-hoc analysis method. They claim that institutions may more focus accurately correlate their risk profile with economic circumstances by explaining the тАЬwhyтАЭ underlying model results, turning a technical forecast into a useful business insight for the advancement.
According to current business viewpoints, autonomous technologies will replace the human interpretation in the next generation of credit risk and any other areas as well. The projected growth of corporate credit, according to McKinsey & Company (2025)[5], is тАЬagentic,тАЭ in which AI agents control the entire credit procedureтАФfrom initial data collection to final decision-making after going through the processтАФall while enforcing an extensive level of accessibility for consumerтАЩs trust.
To go further, Kubam (2024)[6] outlines an algorithm of тАЬAgentic AIтАЭ that permits independent, quick, and understandable making of decisions. As a way to maintain manual human-oriented control over the machine even as it grows increasingly autonomous, these agents are built to track their own outcomes and provide simple analyses of their decision making. This project understands this dynamic horizon as the setting in which powerful, transparent models will ultimately work, even if it concentrates on using LightGBM as the primary estimation algorithm.
Based on the reviewed literature, I think there is still a critical need for frameworks that can handle the particular characteristics of banking information (such as disparities in classes and classification characteristics) without losing accessibility towards customers, even though the financial services sector is quickly trying to move to complicated, high-speed algorithms like LightGBM. As developing a LightGBM pipeline that makes use of Shapley values \((\phi_i)\) to offer a strong auditing trail for credit, this project covers that gap and assures that the algorithm is both тАЬstate-of-the-artтАЭ in terms efficiency and тАЬstate-of-the-compliantтАЭ in terms of accessibility.

title: Explainable Agentic AI for Credit Risk Assessment using LightGBM
text:
Evaluation of credit risk is vital for the financial services industry because, it is required to avoid risks and maintain compliance with regulations, organizations have to accurately predict a borrowerтАЩs likelihood of failure while re-payment. Because they are straightforward and easy to understand for anyone, typical evaluation methods like systems based on rules and regression analysis have been often used in AI world. Modern advancement in machine learning, although have shown that the hierarchical (structural) financial data can now be predicted with significant advantages.
The Risk of default Assessment, which measures a lenderтАЩs probability count of defaulting lenders, it is an important aspect of organizational stability in the technological current days in financial environment. The industry has already started moving from restrictive rule-wise structures to complicated Machine Learning (ML) architectures, as stated by Shreya and Pathak (2025)[1]. Although the fact is these models provide past unheard-of precision in forecasting, they are frequently addressed to as тАЬblack boxes.тАЭ The paper uses Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) to overcome the visibility gap and LightGBM to develop a robust credit assessment engine.
According to author de Lange et al.┬а(2022)[2] and Bussmann et al.┬а(2020)[3], financial businesses should offer a тАЬRight to justificationтАЭ as a way to make sure that risk management is the responsibility for both moral and verifiable. This flexibility is not just a technical preference in organization the but also a legal need. In a similar vein, de Lange et al.┬а(2022)[2] demonstrate that explainable AI methods can maintain accurate predictions while working on to improve an accessibility in financial systems nowadays. The significance of combining the both concepts explainable machine learning with business analytics for ethical evaluation of credit risk defaulters have been demonstrated by recent studies by Shreya and Pathak (2025)[1] and Oyeyemi et al.┬а(2025)[4].
Here are the important steps to develop a machine learning-based credit risk model to build overall good performance:

The first is collecting and preparing the data, including the encrypting variables with categories and finding the missing values.
Dividing the dataset into two subsets for testing and training.
Applying the training data to train for the LightGBM classifier.
Assessing predictive performance for the the model with metrics like ROC-AUC, accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.
Utilizing SHAP-based explanation and characteristic significance for analyzing the model forecasts.

This method provides that the model is easy to understand, versatile, and trustworthy. The predictive modelтАЩs accuracy on unidentified information is determined using model evaluation approaches including cross-validity and train-test partitioning.
This project is using the Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM) programmed in Python langauge to create an Explainable Credit Risk Assessment system, driven by major breakthroughs in both research and business. LightGBM is determined because it performs well on structured table type datasets and is effective and versatile way. The Credit Risk DatasetтАЩs consumer demographic details, banking, and credit historical information will be used by the algorithm to forecast defaults listing on loans potential. Explainable methods like SHAP will be used to understand both specific and worldwide projections in order to assure integrity and transperency.
The objective of this study is to show how explainable artificial intelligence concepts may be integrated with efficient boosting algorithms to create precise, accessible, and insightful credit risk evaluation solutions to avoid the losses of financial organization.

text:
3. Analysis and Results

3.1 Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) :

3.1.1 Data Description :
The Credit Risk Dataset, which includes 32,581 borrower records and 12 characteristics pertaining to financial, credit history, and demographic data, is used in this study. Binary in nature, the target variable loan_status indicates whether a borrower is likely to default (1) or not (0). Income, loan amount, interest rate, length of employment, home ownership status, and loan purpose are among the numerical and qualitative variables included in the dataset. Its reasonable size and realistic structure make it ideal for using LightGBM and assessing explainable, agentic AI decision-making in credit risk prediction.

Dataset Dimensions -
Observations (rows): 32,581
Features (columns): 12
Target Variable: loan_status


Target Variable -
loan_status
1 = High Risk (Default)
0 = Low Risk (Non-Default)



3.1.2 Data Ingestion (Python) :
The pandas package in Python was used to load the dataset. A dataframe called credit1 was created by reading the file credit_risk_dataset.csv. The dataset was first explored by examining column data types, previewing the first few records, and determining its dimensions. To guarantee effective ingestion and comprehend variable composition, the dataset structure was validated using the.shape(),.head(), and.info() functions.

import os
import pandas as pd
from skimpy import skim

def find_git_root(start=None):
    if start is None:
        start = os.getcwd()
    path = os.path.abspath(start)
    
    while True:
        if os.path.isdir(os.path.join(path, ".git")):
            return path
        
        parent = os.path.dirname(path)
        if parent == path:
            raise FileNotFoundError("No .git directory found тАФ are you inside a Git repository?")
        
        path = parent

repo_root = find_git_root()

find_git_root <- function(start = getwd()) {
  path <- normalizePath(start, winslash = "/", mustWork = TRUE)
  while (path != dirname(path)) {
    if (dir.exists(file.path(path, ".git"))) return(path)
    path <- dirname(path)
  }
  stop("No .git directory found тАФ are you inside a Git repository?")

import os
import pandas as pd

def find_git_root(start=None):
    if start is None:
        start = os.getcwd()
    path = os.path.abspath(start)
    
    while True:
        if os.path.isdir(os.path.join(path, ".git")):
            return path
        
        parent = os.path.dirname(path)
        if parent == path:
            raise FileNotFoundError("No .git directory found тАФ are you inside a Git repository?")
        
        path = parent

title: Explainable Agentic AI for Credit Risk Assessment using LightGBM
text:
A vital role for financial institutions is evaluating credit risk, which has a direct impact on lending choices, portfolio performance, and regulatory compliance. Because they are straightforward and easy to understand, traditional credit scoring techniques like logistic regression have gained widespread use. These conventional models, however, find it difficult to account for nonlinear patterns and complicated borrower behaviors as financial data grows in size, complexity, and heterogeneity.
The predictive accuracy of credit risk modeling has greatly increased as a result of recent developments in machine learning, especially ensemble-based techniques like Gradient Boosting Decision Trees (GBDT). Among these techniques, the Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM) has shown itself to be a very effective and scalable framework that can deal with imbalanced and high-dimensional credit datasets. Although LightGBM performs well, it is frequently perceived as a тАЬblack-boxтАЭ paradigm, which restricts transparency and undermines confidence among auditors, regulators, and business users.
This paper suggests an Explainable Agentic AI framework for credit risk assessment utilizing LightGBM in order to overcome these issues. The framework combines an intelligent agentic decision layer that analyzes model predictions, produces insightful explanations, and suggests suitable courses of action with SHAP-based explainability. The suggested approach seeks to facilitate responsible and explicable credit decision-making in regulated financial environments by fusing prediction accuracy, transparency, and adaptive reasoning.

title: Present a great story for data science projects
text:
Introduction
In financial institutions, evaluating credit risk is essential since it has a direct bearing on lending choices, portfolio performance, and regulatory compliance. The simplicity and interpretability of traditional credit scoring models, including logistic regression, have led to their widespread use. Unfortunately, these models are less successful due to the increasing number and complexity of financial data, as they are unable to capture complicated borrower behavior and non-linear correlations. Credit risk prediction has been greatly enhanced by recent developments in machine learning, particularly ensemble techniques like Gradient Boosting Decision Trees. Large, high-dimensional, and unbalanced credit datasets can be handled using the scalable and potent Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM) model. LightGBM is frequently regarded as a black-box approach, which restricts transparency and erodes confidence among regulators and business users despite its impressive performance. This study suggests an Explainable Agentic AI framework for credit risk assessment utilizing LightGBM in order to overcome these issues. An intelligent agentic decision layer that can read predictions, produce explanations, and suggest actions is combined with LightGBM and SHAP-based explainability. The system seeks to promote responsible and explicable credit judgments in regulated financial contexts by combining accuracy, transparency, and adaptive decision-making.

import os
import pandas as pd

def find_git_root(start=None):
    if start is None:
        start = os.getcwd()
    path = os.path.abspath(start)
    
    while True:
        if os.path.isdir(os.path.join(path, ".git")):
            return path
        
        parent = os.path.dirname(path)
        if parent == path:
            raise FileNotFoundError("No .git directory found тАФ are you inside a Git repository?")
        
        path = parent

repo_root = find_git_root()
datasets_path = os.path.join(repo_root, "datasets")
